title: Justice
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Justice
summary: From the prophets to Jesus, Scripture presents justice not as an optional concern but as a core expression of faithfulness to a God who defends the vulnerable and holds the powerful accountable.
body:
Justice is not a secondary theme in Scripture — it is central to God's character. The psalmist declares that righteousness and justice are the foundation of God's throne [verse:ps.89.14]. When God acts, he acts justly. When he calls his people, he calls them to the same.

The prophets made this devastatingly clear. Amos confronts Israel's religious observance while the poor are exploited: God despises their festivals and refuses their offerings because justice has been abandoned [verse:amos.5.21-24]. Let justice roll down like waters, he thunders — not trickle, not appear occasionally, but flow ceaselessly. Isaiah echoes the same priority: the fasting God chooses is to loose the bonds of injustice and let the oppressed go free [verse:isa.58.6-7].

Jesus identifies himself with the vulnerable in the parable of the sheep and goats. Whatever you did for the least of these, you did for me [verse:matt.25.40]. Justice is not abstract — it is expressed in feeding the hungry, clothing the naked, visiting the imprisoned.

Proverbs frames injustice toward the poor as an affront to God himself: whoever oppresses the poor shows contempt for their Maker [verse:prov.14.31]. The call to justice is simultaneously a call to worship — you cannot honor the Creator while devaluing the creatures who bear his image.

Micah's summary remains the clearest distillation: what does the Lord require? To do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God [verse:mic.6.8].

verses.annotation: God rejects worship that coexists with injustice — let justice roll like a river.
God's requirements distilled to three: justice, mercy, and humble walking with him.
Justice and righteousness as the very foundation of God's throne.
True fasting is loosing injustice and caring for the oppressed, not merely abstaining from food.
Jesus identifies with the vulnerable — service to them is service to him.
Oppressing the poor is an insult to their Maker; kindness to the needy honors God.
A divine command to defend the weak and fatherless and uphold the cause of the afflicted.

scholars.quote: Stuart reads Amos 5 as the prophet's most devastating critique: Israel's elaborate worship was not merely insufficient but offensive to God because it functioned as a substitute for the justice he actually demanded.
Oswalt argues that Isaiah 58 redefines religious devotion — genuine fasting is measured not by personal discipline but by its fruit in the lives of the oppressed.
Longman observes that Proverbs consistently treats the poor as God's special concern, making economic exploitation not merely a social issue but a theological offense.
